coredata.dc:title: Semiempirical study on the absorption spectra of the coronene-like molecular models of graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Budyka, Mikhail F.
coredata.dc:description:
Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons of the general formula C6n
2H6n (coronene family) were used as molecular models of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Absorption spectra of the model compounds were calculated by ZINDO/S method. The S0 → S1 transition energy (E 1) was found to decrease with n as E 1 = 4.75 × n −0.633 eV. This transition is forbidden in symmetric compounds but ‘switches on’ upon symmetry breaking. The energy of the first bright optical peak (E br) was found to decrease with n as E br = 6.31 × n −0.6 eV. The data obtained corroborate the earlier finding that the size-independent optical properties of GQDs are determined by relatively small isolated sp 2 clusters separated by sp 3 (oxygen-contained) ‘defects’ rather than the whole (corrupted) graphene sheets; such nanoparticles actually are not quantum dots. GQDs of pure (without defects) graphene sheets with fully π-conjugated sp
2 systems should exhibit size-dependent optical properties due to the quantum confinement effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coronene
Graphene quantum dot
Cluster
Absorption
ZINDO/S

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structures of the model compounds of the coronene family.

Calculated (ZINDO/S) energy of the first singlet (optically dark) transition E 1 and the first optically bright transition E br as a function of the homologous index n for the coronene family of general formula C6n
2H6n , the molecule size is also shown. The solid symbols represent calculated data, the lines are the fit to the hyperbola.
Isomers of the model epoxy-compound C54H18O; the sp 3 defect regions are shown by light-gray.
Properties of GQDs synthesized via different synthetic methods.

Calculated parameters of the model compounds of the coronene family C6n
2H6n : the van der Waals size, the energies of the S0 → S1 transition (E 1) and the lowest optically bright transition (E br).

head.ce:title: Semiempirical study on the absorption spectra of the coronene-like molecular models of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Problems of Chemical Physics, Russian Academy of Sciences, 142432 Chernogolovka, Moscow Region, Russian Federation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Problems of Chemical Physics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons of the general formula C6n
2H6n (coronene family) were used as molecular models of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Absorption spectra of the model compounds were calculated by ZINDO/S method. The S0 → S1 transition energy (E 1) was found to decrease with n as E 1 = 4.75 × n −0.633 eV. This transition is forbidden in symmetric compounds but ‘switches on’ upon symmetry breaking. The energy of the first bright optical peak (E br) was found to decrease with n as E br = 6.31 × n −0.6 eV. The data obtained corroborate the earlier finding that the size-independent optical properties of GQDs are determined by relatively small isolated sp 2 clusters separated by sp 3 (oxygen-contained) ‘defects’ rather than the whole (corrupted) graphene sheets; such nanoparticles actually are not quantum dots. GQDs of pure (without defects) graphene sheets with fully π-conjugated sp
2 systems should exhibit size-dependent optical properties due to the quantum confinement effect.

ce:section.ce:para: The fundamental building block of all graphene materials is a graphene layer, which, according to the recommended nomenclature, is defined as ‘a single-atom-thick sheet of hexagonally arranged, sp 2-bonded carbon atoms occurring within a carbon material structure’ [1].

Graphene quantum dot (GQD) consists of a single to several layers of graphene sheets several nanometers in lateral size. Graphene composed fully of the sp 2-hybridized carbon atoms exhibits no fluorescence due to a zero optical bandgap. Compared to ‘infinite’ graphene sheet, experimentally obtained GQDs are limited in size and always contain both the sp 2 and sp 3 carbon atoms (and heteroatoms); both these factors open the optical bandgap (splitting valence and conduction bands to discrete molecular orbitals) and result in fluorescence.

Apart from the ability to emit light (photo- and electroluminescence), GQDs are inexpensive, nontoxic, photostable, water-soluble, biocompatible, and environmentally friendly objects. Due to the combination of these remarkable properties, GQDs can find possible numerous applications as photodetectors, solar cells and light-emitting diodes [2,3], chemo- and bio-sensors [4–8], other biomedical applications (bioimaging, drug delivery, anticancer therapy) [9–11], devices for measuring pH [12].

However, many authors note that ‘the widespread use of GQDs is hindered by the poor understanding of their photoluminescence mechanism’ [13]. Despite intensive studies and many reports on the light-emitting properties of GQDs, the actual mechanism of the GQDs fluorescence is still not completely understood, remains controversial and is a subject of active discussions [2,14–22].

One of the problems is that the emission wavelength of GQDs is often dependent on the excitation wavelength, in contrast to organic dyes (and inorganic QDs), where the fluorescence spectrum is independent of the excitation wavelength because fluorescence starts from the lowest relaxed excited state (Kasha's rule [23]). Another problem is that the absorption and emission spectra are often independent of the size of the GQDs, i.e., they do not follow the quantum confinement effect, in contrast to the semiconductor QDs such as CdS, InAs, ets, [24–26]. For example, GQDs prepared by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method have sizes from 1.65 to 21 nm, but neither absorption (228 and 282 nm) nor emission (near 500 nm when excited at 395 nm) wavelengths exhibit dependence on the size of the GQDs [27].

On the other hand, the 3 nm uniform-sized GQDs prepared by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite have an absorption peak at 227 nm and excitation wavelength-independent red emission at 610 nm [28], whereas GQDs of the same size (3 nm) fabricated by a hydrothermal cutting method have an absorption shoulder at 420 nm and an emission peak at 500 nm that shifts with excitation wavelength [29]. Some representative examples of the GQDs optical properties are in Table 1 ; more comprehensive data can be found in the recent reviews [9,16,30].

In Table 1, the I D/I G parameter is the intensity ratio of two characteristic bands in the Raman spectra. The disordered D band (~1350 cm−1) is related to the breathing mode of the aromatic rings induced by the sp 3 defects; the crystalline (graphitic) G band (~1590 cm−1) is associated with the in-plane vibration (the bond stretching) of the sp 2 bonded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice [14,16,30]. The I D/I G ratio is used to characterize the quality or degree of disorder of the carbon nanoparticles. In this respect, one should differentiate between GQDs and carbon dots (C-dots). GQDs with the high degree of graphitization have relatively low I D/I G ratio, amorphous C-dots have higher I D/I G ratio [36]. GQDs and C-dots differ also by shape: GQDs resemble flat disks, whereas C-dots are quasi-spherical particles [19,30,37,38].

Various theoretical approaches have been used to explain the optical properties of GQDs [39–47]. The effects of different geometries such as square, rectangle, and triangle, lateral size, edge structure, corner geometry, defects, heteroatoms have been studied. Depending on the symmetry of GQD, the dark nature of the lowest singlet transitions and the degeneracy of the lowest optically bright transition have been found [40,44,45]. Using a combination of tight-binding approach with a self-consistent Hartree-Fock method and taking into account correlation and excitonic effects, it has been shown that the optical gap decreases as the inverse of the square root of the number of atoms in the armchair-edged hexagonal GQDs [39]. On the other hand, a discrete dipole approximation has predicted that the optical extinction peak of monolayer graphene firstly significantly red shifts with increasing diameter but then tends to a saturation value when the diameter is above 20 nm, showing a strong small-size-sensitive effect [42].

In the present paper, the effect of size on optical properties of GQDs has been studied using a series of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) from the coronene family. Different PAHs are often used as molecular models of GQDs [48–52]. It is obvious because any PAH consisting of fused hexagonal rings can be regarded as a piece of graphene sheet where all dangling bonds at the edge are saturated with hydrogen atoms. For the spectroscopic calculations, we have chosen the semiempirical INDO/S method, which has proven to reproduce main features of GQDs predicted by higher-level methods: dark nature of the lowest singlet states, degeneracy of the lowest optically bright transition, bathochromic shift of the absorption bands with molecule size, and convergence to zero of the optical gap. Moreover, the quantitative correlation between the transition energy and molecule homologous index n has been found.

The ground state geometry was first optimized by semiempirical AM1 (Austin Model 1) method [53]. The vibrational spectra were computed and had no imaginary frequencies. Using AM1-optimized geometry, absorption spectra were calculated by ZINDO/S (Zerner's-intermediate-neglect-of-differential-overlap-for-spectroscopy) method including up to 60 highest occupied and 60 lowest virtual orbitals. This method has been parameterized to reproduce namely spectroscopic data and is used for calculating electronic spectra [54,55]. All calculations were performed with the Gaussian 09 program package [56] using standard convergence criteria.

The general formula of the coronene family is C6n
2H6n , where homologous index n is a positive integer and characterizes a number of linearly cata-condensed benzene rings on the molecular radius from the center to the perimeter of a molecule. Formally, the first member of the series (n = 1) is benzene C6H6, a starting point for construction of any PAH, and coronene C24H12 is the second member. Fig. 1 shows the structures of the first six members of the series.

All calculated compounds have twofold degenerate frontier orbitals, the highest occupied (HOMO, H) and the lowest unoccupied (LUMO, L) molecular orbitals. The lowest excited singlet states (S1) arise from a combination of H → L + 1 and H-1 → L electron promotions. The S0 → S1 transitions are symmetry-forbidden and correspond to the optically dark states. In the experimental absorption spectra, the lowest wavelength absorption bands are very weak [57,58].

Above the dark states, there are optically bright ones. For coronene and higher homologues, the lowest bright transitions are twofold degenerate and correspond to the excitations to the S6 and S7 states with the involvement of electron promotions between the frontier molecular orbitals, H → L + 1 and H-1 → L, H → L and H-1 → L + 1, respectively.
The calculated energies of the S0 → S1 transitions E 1 and the lowest optically bright transitions E br are collected in Table 2 . Both E 1 and E br decrease with the molecule size.
Fig. 2 shows the transition energies E 1 and E br as a function of the homologous index n for the coronene family; the known experimental data for benzene [57] and coronene [58] are also depicted.

The dependence observed can be fitted by the hyperbolic function (Eq. (1)).
(1)
E = a ×
n b
eV
where, for the first singlet transition (E 1), a 1 = 4.75 ± 0.01 and b 1 = −0.633 ± 0.003 (correlation coefficient R = 0.9999), and for the first optically bright transition (E br), a br = 6.31 ± 0.09 and b br = −0.60 ± 0.02 (R = 0.9984).

The data obtained can be compared with the earlier DFT calculations. Depending on the structure of the molecular models and the methods used, different correlations have been obtained but, independently of the structure and the method, the absorption (luminescence) band energy decreases (the wavelength increases) as the compound size increases.

Using the first-principle DFT based many body perturbation theory and the hexagonal models, Kozawa et al. have found that the energy of the first bright peak in the absorption spectrum is a linear function of the inverse size [59]. Sk et al. have calculated (at the B3LYP/6-31G* level) that the emission of zigzag-edged pristine GQDs can cover the entire visible light spectrum by varying the diameter from 0.89 to 1.80 nm, the calculated emission wavelength being linearly dependent on the GQD diameter [13]. Using the same DFT method, Zhang et al. have examined the size effect in a series of square GQDs and found that the shift of the strongest absorption peak shows a square root dependence on the total number of carbon and hydrogen atoms [60]. In the linearly condensed acene series with the number of benzene rings up to n = 8, the absorption band wavelength increases linearly with n for the L a state, whereas for the L b state a nonlinear dependence with an asymptotic limit has been found [61].
According to Eq. (1), both E 1 and E br decrease to 0 as n → ∞ that corresponds to the fact that graphene has zero optical bandgap.

As above noted, calculations predict zero oscillator strengths for the S0 → S1 transitions since they are symmetry-forbidden. To evaluate the effect of symmetry, which hardly exists in small graphene flakes obtained by an accidental break of the graphene sheet, we removed two adjacent carbon atoms from the molecule perimeter of coronene C24H12 (symmetry D6h) and obtained benzo[ghi]perylene (or 1,12-benzoperylene) C22H12 (symmetry Cs). In this compound, as compared to coronene, the calculated band energies changed slightly and in different directions, for example, from 3.07 to 3.23 eV for the S1 band, and from 3.35 to 3.29 eV for the S2 band, whereas the oscillator strengths for these bands (zero in coronene) increased to 0.0024 and 0.3989 for the S1 and S2 band, respectively.

Thus, taking into account the data obtained and the results of the model calculations by different theoretical methods [13,22,40,43,45,62], one should expect that small graphene flakes (some nanometers in size) have absorption bands in the visible region.

The chemically synthesized graphene models with precise molecular structures really absorb in visible region [48], for example, absorption bands maxima are 490 nm for C96H30 [51], 491 nm for C80H30 [63], 575 and 621 nm for C150H42 [64], 591 nm (with tail up to 900 nm) for C168H42 [65].

However, the experimental absorption spectra of GQDs are restricted by the UV – short-wavelength visible region (see examples in Table 1); moreover, luminescence in the visible region denies the existence of absorption bands with low energy (for the same molecule, excluding emission from the higher excited states [66]).

The cause of a discrepancy between the calculated absorption spectra of the model compounds and the experimental spectra of GQDs can be discovered taking into account the methods of GQD preparation. These methods are basically divided into two classes: top-down and bottom-up [9,15,30]. The top-down approach consists in a decomposition of the large-sized carbonaceous precursors (reducing the size), upon bottom-up approach GQDs are prepared from the small molecular precursors by a step-wise chemical synthesis (increasing the size). In practice, the most popular and often used is the top-down approach when GQDs are obtained by graphene oxidation to graphene oxide (GO) followed by reduction [16,52,62,67]. GO has a characteristic absorption band at ~230 nm due to the π–π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds with a tail extending to the visible range, and a broad band at 270–390 nm (or a shoulder at ~300 nm) due to n–π* transitions of CO bonds [62]. Upon the GO reduction (by different methods), the π–π* peak shifts to ~270 nm, the n–π* peak disappears, and the absorption in the whole (UV–vis) spectral region increases with reaction time leading to a gradual color change from brownish-yellow to black [68–70]. This is a result of the gradual restoration of the π-π electronic conjugation in the oxidized graphene sheets (sp 3 → sp 2 rehybridization) and corresponds to the fact that the final product of GO reduction is graphene.

However, in the most cases, the reduction does not remove all oxygen from GO [67]; to underline this, a technical name ‘reduced graphene oxide’ (rGO) rather than ‘simply’ graphene is ordinary used [52]. Therefore, GQDs, prepared from GO, due to incomplete reduction, always have some oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl, epoxy, ether, carbonyl, carboxylic), which break the full chain of the π-conjugation in the graphene sheet plane and create the rather small sp 2 clusters separated by the remained sp 3 inclusions. Even provided using a new method - sonication exfoliation without the special stage of oxidation - but nevertheless the percentage of carbonyl increases from ~0% to ~27% [35].

The mechanisms of GO and GQD luminescence are discussed in the literature [13,16,71], however, it is clear that namely isolated sp 2 clusters (with possible conjugation with adjacent remained oxygen-containing groups) are emitting centers (sp 3 structures do not emit) [62]. The size and topology of these emitting aromatic ‘islands’ determine the emission wavelength. As a result, the optical properties of GQDs consisting of partially reduced GO sheets do not depend on the GQDs size since they are determined by properties of isolated sp 2 clusters [16]. At the same time, the top-down synthesized GQDs often exhibit excitation-dependent and broad emission spectra, because of the heterogeneity in size and shape of the sp 2 clusters, composition of the functional groups, defects, etc. [13]. For example, an addition of one oxygen atom to circumcoronene C54H18 with the formation of an epoxy group creates an sp 3 defect of two carbon atoms in the π-conjugated sp 2 system. The epoxy-compound C54H18O can have different structures, three possible isomers are shown in Fig. 3 . We calculated absorption spectra for these isomers. Depending on the position of the epoxy group and, accordingly, on the topology of the conjugated sp 2 cluster, calculations predict different λabs values: 486, 594 and 513 nm for the isomer A, B, and C, respectively (for the starting circumcoronene λabs = 528 nm, Table 2).

As above mentioned, the intensity ratio of the Raman D and G bands is a known measure of the size of the sp 2 regions in a network of the sp 3- and sp 2-bonded carbon. We can estimate the average sizes of the sp 2 clusters using the empirical Tuinstra–Koenig [72] relation between the I D/I G ratio and the size of sp 2 clusters (named crystallites in [72]). For example, ‘small’ GQDs of 3–5 nm [32], as well as ‘big’ GQDs of 30–40 nm [35] have the same I D/I G = 1.3 (Table 1). Therefore, they have the same sp 2 clusters with an average size of ~ 3.3 nm.

Provided complete reduction and restoration of the π-π conjugation plane through all graphene flake, the flake as a whole determines the GQD absorption and luminescence; therefore such GQDs show size-dependent optical properties due to the quantum confinement effect [16,51].

DFT and TDDFT calculations reveal that the GQDs optical properties depend not only on the size but also on edge configuration and shape [40,43,62,73]; additionally, for modified GQDs, the properties depend on attached chemical functionalities, heteroatom doping and defects [13,59,74]. For the pristine zigzag-edged GQDs with different diameters, TDDFT predicts the linear dependence of the emission wavelength on the size from deep UV (235.2 nm) to near infrared (999.5 nm) on varying the size from 0.46 nm (C6H6) to 2.31 nm (C150H30) [13]. For the experimentally bottom-up synthesized GQDs of various shapes but with exact structures (C132H38, C168H42, and C170H42), TDDFT predicts correctly red emission [13].

The ideal (unspoiled) graphene sheet with fully conjugated π-system does not fluoresce because of zero bandgap. To observe the fluorescence, one should destroy the ideal pattern of the graphene honeycomb lattice to obtain emitting isolated sp 2 clusters or reduce the size of the graphene sheet in such a way that it becomes itself a restricted sp 2 cluster rather than the unlimited plane (to open bandgap and switch on the quantum confinement effect).

Absorption spectra of the coronene family C6n
2H6n were calculated by ZINDO/S method. The calculated energy of the lowest singlet symmetry-forbidden optically dark transition was found to decrease as a hyperbolic function of the homologous index n. The same dependence was found for the energy of the first optically bright transition. Comparison of the data obtained with the earlier published experimental and theoretical studies of GQDs and model polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons allows us to make the following general conclusions.

Optical properties of the GQDs comprising pristine graphene sheets where every layer is a fully π-conjugated sp
2 cluster without defects can be controlled by the QD size due to the quantum confinement effect. This refers to the bottom-up synthesized GQDs (graphene molecular models) with atomically precise chemical structure, their absorption and emission bands shift to the longer wavelengths with size increasing. For the top-down synthesized GQDs, the observed short-wavelength absorption bands are defined by separate rather small sp
2 clusters. These GQDs have no definite structure and often exhibit excitation-dependent emission spectra since they are mixtures of different QDs heterogeneous in size, shape, edge configuration, sp 3-defects, oxygen functional groups. Due to this, optical properties of the partially reduced GQDs can be controlled by the extent of reduction that offers possibilities to tailor these properties using different reducing agent and/or treatment procedure (concentration, temperature, time, etc.).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author wish to thank Professor E.F. Sheka for stimulating discussions.
